title: Today's AI Brief: Why Google’s AI can’t spell Google (or anything else)
summary: Google's AI struggles with spelling, Illinois passes AI safety bill, and more
imageAlt: Image from source article for Why Google’s AI can’t spell Google (or anything else)
imageCaption: Why Google’s AI can’t spell Google (or anything else)
imageSourceTitle: Why Google’s AI can’t spell Google (or anything else)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of AI. Let's dive in. I've been following a couple of news items that caught my attention, and I thought I'd share them with you.

So, how many Ps are in Google? According to Google's AI, there are two. Yeah, that's not correct. The AI also thinks there's exactly one 'r' in the word 'poop', and two 'd's in journalism, but can't quite get the spelling right. This is a bit of a funny example of how AI models can struggle with basic tasks. The article from TechCrunch suggests that this might be due to the way these models are trained on vast amounts of data, which can sometimes lead to... creative... spellings. It's a lighthearted reminder that AI models are still learning and improving.

In more serious news, Illinois lawmakers have just passed a bill that's being hailed as one of the strongest AI safety laws in the US. The bill requires major AI labs like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind to have their safety practices audited by a third party. This is a big deal, as it shows that governments are starting to take AI safety seriously and trying to ensure that these powerful models are developed and used responsibly. It's an interesting step towards accountability in the AI world.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
